original_abstract: This research investigates the psychological and behavioral development of students at SMP Nidaul Quran, Sarolangun, focusing on how discipline and religious values foster resilience and a sense of responsibility. Employing a qualitative case study design, data were collected through in-depth interviews with students, educators, and parents, supplemented by field observations and thematic document analysis. The findings reveal that discipline serves as a fundamental pillar for character building, moving beyond mere rule enforcement to facilitate the internalisation of integrity and self-control. The study highlights a successful synergy between behavioral standards and Islamic spiritual practices, such as congregational prayers and Quranic recitation, which strengthens students' moral fortitude and emotional resilience. Furthermore, the results underscore the vital role of teachers as moral exemplars and the necessity of active parental involvement in reinforcing school values at home. The study concludes that an integrated, holistic approach to Islamic education—incorporating structured discipline and spiritual cultivation—provides a robust framework for developing well-rounded individuals capable of navigating modern challenges with ethical integrity. This model offers scalable insights for other Islamic educational institutions seeking to balance academic competence with deep-seated character development.
humanized_abstract: The purpose of this study was to examine the development of student’s psychological and behaviour at SMP Nidaul Quran, Sarolangun. The main focus of the study was to identify the role of discipline and religious values in building student’s resilience and responsibility. Therefore, the qualitative case study research approach was employed. The data were collected through in-depth interviews with student, teacher and parents, as well as through field observation and thematic document analysis. The result of the study indicated that the discipline was the backbone of building student’s character which was not limited to obedience but also referred to student’s integrity and self-control. The discipline was strongly related to the religion where student’s Islamic practices, such as ‘ishoma (performing congregational prayer) and reading the Quran, had helped in strengthening their morale and psychological resilience. The study further revealed that teachers needed to exemplify Islamic values, and parents should reinforce the school values at home in order to achieve students’ conformity to the discipline at school. Thus, the study concluded that the structural discipline and spiritual educational approach, which formed the basis of the Islamic educational system, was effective in nurturing students’ global competence and characters. Therefore, the study’s finding has implications for other Islamic educational institutions to develop students’ competence and characters through the values of discipline and spiritual education.
